questions.question: A host on subnet 10.1.0.0/24 wants to reach a host on 10.2.0.0/24. Both subnets are connected to a router. The sending host sends the packet but it never arrives. What is the MOST likely missing configuration?
options: The destination host does not have a firewall exception
The router does not have IP forwarding enabled
The sending host needs a DNS entry for the destination
The subnets are too small to communicate across a router
questions.explanation: On Linux, IP forwarding is disabled by default. A router with correct routes will still silently drop packets between interfaces if net.ipv4.ip_forward=0. This is one of the most common misconfiguration mistakes. The fix is `sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1`. Routing table entries alone are not enough.

questions.question: A routing table has two entries that match the same destination: 10.0.0.0/8 and 10.1.0.0/16. Which one is selected for a packet to 10.1.2.3?
options: 10.0.0.0/8 because it was added first
10.1.0.0/16 because it has the longer (more specific) prefix
Both routes are used in round-robin order
Neither — the router drops the packet when there is ambiguity
questions.explanation: Routers use longest prefix match: the route with the most network bits wins. 10.1.0.0/16 has 16 network bits; 10.0.0.0/8 has 8. Both match 10.1.2.3, but /16 is more specific. The order routes were added does not matter. This algorithm is fundamental to how the internet works — a host route (/32) always beats a subnet route (/24) which beats a default route (/0).

questions.question: In the lab, what is the purpose of the `ip netns exec router` prefix before every router configuration command?
options: It grants root privileges only within the router namespace
It runs the command inside the router network namespace, affecting only that namespace's interfaces and routing table
It logs the command output to the router namespace's journal
It prevents the command from affecting the host's network configuration
questions.explanation: Network namespaces are completely isolated networking environments. Without `ip netns exec router`, any `ip` command would modify the host (root) namespace's interfaces and routing table — not the router namespace. The `exec` subcommand is how you 'enter' a namespace to run commands within its context. This is exactly analogous to how containers isolate their network stacks.

questions.question: After the lab setup, `ip netns exec hostA ip route show` displays: `default via 10.0.1.1 dev veth-a`. What does this mean?
options: hostA's only interface is 10.0.1.1
hostA will send all packets for unknown destinations to 10.0.1.1 via the veth-a interface
All traffic on veth-a is blocked except to 10.0.1.1
10.0.1.1 is the DNS server for hostA
questions.explanation: The `default` route matches 0.0.0.0/0 — every possible destination. `via 10.0.1.1` means the next hop (gateway) is 10.0.1.1 (the router). `dev veth-a` is the outgoing interface. So when hostA sends to 10.0.2.2 (which has no more-specific route), it consults `default`, sends the packet to 10.0.1.1 on veth-a, and the router forwards it to hostB.

questions.question: What is a veth pair and why is it used in this lab?
options: A VLAN-tagged Ethernet interface that spans multiple subnets
Two virtual interfaces connected back-to-back; packets sent into one end emerge from the other — used to link network namespaces
A pair of physical NICs bonded together for redundancy
A software bridge that copies packets between two namespaces
questions.explanation: A veth (virtual Ethernet) pair is created as a matched pair: packets entering one end immediately emerge from the other, like a virtual cable. By creating a veth pair and moving one end into each namespace (e.g., one end into hostA, the other into router), we create an isolated link between them. This simulates a physical cable connecting two machines without any real hardware.
